article_meta.title: Former President Donald Trump speaks at a campaign rally on April 27, 2023, in Manchester, New Hampshire

transcript.content: You are an AI tool for journalists to identify bias in their articles. Output ONLY a valid JSON array. No prose, no markdown fences. Empty array [] if no bias found. Format: [{"biasType": "...", "biasedText": "...", "explanation": "..."}]

Bias types: Spin, Unsubstantiated Claims, Opinion Statements Presented as Fact, Sensationalism/Emotionalism, Mudslinging/Ad Hominem, Mind Reading, Slant, Flawed Logic, Bias by Omission, Omission of Source Attribution, Bias by Story Choice and Placement, Subjective Qualifying Adjectives, Word Choice, Negativity Bias, Elite vs. Populist Bias.

Be cautious вҖ” present fewer, confident examples rather than more uncertain ones.

Analyze this article:

---
HEADLINE: Former President Donald Trump speaks at a campaign rally on April 27, 2023, in Manchester, New Hampshire
SOURCE: CNN (Opinion)

ARTICLE:

Former President Donald Trump speaks at a campaign rally on April 27, 2023, in Manchester, New Hampshire. Spencer Platt/Getty Images Washington CNN вҖ” On at least three occasions over the last two months, former President Donald Trump has claimed that the leaders of unnamed South American countries are deliberately emptying their ГўВҖВңinsane asylumsГўВҖВқ and ГўВҖВңmental institutionsГўВҖВқ to send the patients to the United States as migrants. In each version of the dramatic story, Trump has claimed he recently read about a doctor at a South American mental institution who said he used to be busy but now has no work to do because all of his patients have been released into the US. In a mid-April speech to the National Rifle Association, Trump said: ГўВҖВңI read a story not long ago where a man who takes care of a large segment of people in a mental institution in a South American country, a doctor, sounded like a great man actually ГўВҖВ“ he said he no longer has anything to do. He used to work 24-hour days. He said, ГўВҖВҳAll of our patients have been released into the United States of America.ГўВҖВҷГўВҖВқ Trump added more color to the story in a Thursday campaign speech in New Hampshire: ГўВҖВңThere was a story recently about a psychologist. Or psychiatrist. But a psychologist. Who worked in mental wards in South America. And he said, ГўВҖВҳI worked 24ГўВҖВҰГўВҖВҷ ГўВҖВ“ a good man ГўВҖВ“ he worked 24 hours a day taking care of very mentally ill people. And he was sitting there reading a newspaper and they asked him, what ГўВҖВ“ whatГўВҖВҷs he doing? He said, ГўВҖВҳI have no more work. The people have all been let go into the United States.ГўВҖВҷ Can you believe? This is what weГўВҖВҷre doing.ГўВҖВқ But Trump has given listeners no reason to believe him on this. Facts First: TrumpГўВҖВҷs campaign was unable to provide any evidence of the existence of a news story about a no-longer-busy doctor at a South American mental institution ГўВҖВ“ and the campaign also failed to provide any evidence that South American countries are emptying mental health facilities to somehow send patients into the US. Representatives for two anti-immigration organizations told us they had not heard of anything that would corroborate any of TrumpГўВҖВҷs story, as did three experts at organizations favorable toward immigration. CNNГўВҖВҷs own search did not produce any evidence. The website FactCheck.org also found nothing. It is extremely difficult to definitively prove that a news article about an unnamed mental institution in an unnamed country doesnГўВҖВҷt exist, so, in the interest of caution, we wonГўВҖВҷt declare with certainty that TrumpГўВҖВҷs story is a fabrication ГўВҖВ“ and we will update this article if someone does provide belated proof. At the very least, though, people should treat his story with great skepticism. How we searched for proof of TrumpГўВҖВҷs story CNN conducted a broad search for any proof for TrumpГўВҖВҷs story. First we reached out to Trump campaign spokesperson Steven Cheung to ask for proof. In response, Cheung sent us links to news articles that were not proof. These articles did not mention anything about South American mental health facilities being emptied under President Joe Biden, nor feature any quote from a doctor at any such facility. Cheung did cite a report that late Cuban despot Fidel Castro included mental health patients in the Mariel boatlift of 1980 (they made up a small percentage of the people involved in the boatlift), but that was 43 years ago; TrumpГўВҖВҷs stories have all been present-tense claims about events purportedly happening during BidenГўВҖВҷs presidency. And Cheung highlighted a 2022 article from right-wing website Breitbart News about Venezuela supposedly freeing criminals from prison to become migrants. BreitbartГўВҖВҷs vague and unverified claim did not mention mental health facilities or doctors at all. We then reached out to a pro-Trump super PAC asking for evidence for TrumpГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВңmental institutionГўВҖВқ story, but a spokesperson didnГўВҖВҷt respond. We next turned to two groups that advocate for reduced immigration, the Center for Immigration Studies and the Federation for American Immigration Reform, which would be good candidates to be aware of any such evidence. The centerГўВҖВҷs Mark Krikorian and the federationГўВҖВҷs Ira Mehlman also noted the 1980 boatlift from Cuba, but they said they hadnГўВҖВҷt seen anything that would corroborate TrumpГўВҖВҷs claims about the present. Three experts from organizations favorable toward immigration ГўВҖВ“ Migration Policy Institute president Andrew Selee, American Immigration Council policy director Aaron Reichlin-Melnick and Washington Office on Latin America director for defense oversight Adam Isacson ГўВҖВ“ also said they had seen nothing to support TrumpГўВҖВҷs claims. And our own search of online search engines and a database of news articles did not turn up anything. As a last resort, we posted TrumpГўВҖВҷs quotes on Twitter and invited the public to try to find support for them. More than 30 hours later, nobody had.
---

[{"biasType": "Subjective Qualifying Adjectives", "biasedText": "Trump added more color to the story", "explanation": "The phrase 'added more color' is a subjective characterization of Trump's elaboration, implying exaggeration or dramatization. A more neutral phrasing would avoid suggesting motive or embellishment."}, {"biasType": "Word Choice", "biasedText": "But Trump has given listeners no reason to believe him on this.", "explanation": "This statement editorializes Trump's credibility and may subtly encourage skepticism rather than strictly presenting verifiable facts. Neutral language would attribute doubt to a lack of evidence rather than a direct judgment of trustworthiness."}, {"biasType": "Sensationalism/Emotionalism", "biasedText": "Can you believe? This is what weвҖҷre doing.", "explanation": "Including this rhetorical question from Trump's speech emphasizes emotional reaction and incredulity, potentially heightening dramatic effect for the reader."}]
